ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to strawberry shortcake; A00KY (Cakes and pastries) used as the closest defensible broader category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for strawberry shortcake as a food product; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical slice of strawberry shortcake weighs approximately 120 g, representing a standard dessert portion served in cafes and bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: Strawberry shortcake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values preserved as supplied. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, vitamin C, and other micronutrients not reported in the HPB record; set to -1 where not available, with the exception of calcium and iron which are reported.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sponge Cake and Cream-Based Pastries — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for strawberry shortcake exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 (medium) is estimated from sponge cake and cream-filled pastry analogues published in the University of Sydney GI Database (sponge cake GI ~46–67 range; cream-filled pastry GI ~38–65). The value of 67 represents the upper defensible bound given the added sugar content (11.5 g/100g) and refined flour base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 23.04) / 100 ≈ 15.5, using 120 g serving size (carbohydrate_g = 19.2 × 1.2 = 23.04 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (11.5 g/100g) and estimated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g); sodium is relatively low at 52 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low (52 mg/100g) and does not directly elevate blood pressure, but the high sugar and saturated fat load can contribute to weight gain and arterial stiffness; individuals with hypertension should limit portion to a small slice (≤60 g) and avoid frequent consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (11.5 g/100g), medium-high GI (67), and moderate glycemic load per serving (15.5) from refined flour and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of 11.5 g sugar/100g, a GI of 67, and a glycemic load of 15.5 per 120 g serving produces a significant postprandial glucose spike; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit to a maximum of 50 g per occasion and pair with a protein or fibre source to blunt glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g from butter and whipping cream, combined with high added sugar (11.5 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at approximately 7.5 g/100g exceeds the recommended per-serving threshold for cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence, limit to one small slice (≤60 g), and prioritise lower-fat, lower-sugar dessert alternatives in their regular diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g (above 5 g threshold) and refined sugar 11.5 g/100g promote pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Butter and cream-derived saturated fat can upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 pathways relevant to RA; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption and limit to very small portions on rare occasions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Strawberry shortcake is a classic Western dessert consisting of a light, airy sponge cake base layered with fresh strawberries and whipped cream. The sponge is typically made from wheat flour, eggs, sugar, and butter, baked until golden and tender, then split horizontally and filled generously with sliced or whole fresh strawberries macerated in sugar and topped with lightly sweetened whipping cream. The texture is soft and moist from the cream, with a slight crumb from the sponge and a fresh, slightly tart contrast from the strawberries. The flavour profile is sweet and creamy with bright fruity notes. Per 100 g, strawberry shortcake provides 217 kcal, 3.1 g protein, 14.23 g fat (of which approximately 7.5 g is saturated), 19.2 g carbohydrate, 11.5 g sugar, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 52 mg sodium. The high sugar and saturated fat content make it a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease. The medium-high glycemic index of approximately 67 and a glycemic load of 15.5 per 120 g serving indicate a meaningful postprandial glucose response. Calcium (43 mg/100g) and iron (0.77 mg/100g) are present in modest amounts. This dessert is popular in Singapore cafes and bakeries, often served as a snack or celebratory treat, and is widely recognised across Western and Asian-Western fusion food cultures.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of strawberry shortcake?
Is strawberry shortcake high in sugar?
What is the GI of strawberry shortcake?
Is strawberry shortcake suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in strawberry shortcake?
What are the ingredients in strawberry shortcake?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all reported fields. Saturated fat estimated from known composition of butter and whipping cream components consistent with the food description; this single estimated field does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong HPB anchor for all primary macros. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source record): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for strawberry shortcake as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g (7.5 g/100g) is estimated from the known composition of butter and whipping cream as primary fat contributors in a standard strawberry shortcake recipe; this is consistent with the total fat of 14.23 g/100g and is documented as an estimate. GI value of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using sponge cake and cream-filled pastry reference foods; the estimation basis is fully documented in gi_source. No zero-macro justification is required as all core macros (protein_g 3.1, fat_g 14.23, carbohydrate_g 19.2) are non-zero. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under the multilingual naming rules.
